speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Agnes Healy Jones took place at Latah Convalescent Center on May 6, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: My grandmother said, of course, she died early in life. But anyway, when she was living, mother used to come here to the place and they'd get the first meet you see. Grandpa would drive 30 miles to close for the provisions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Indians, did they find it harder to get their canvas once the land was starting to be farmed? Didn't they take a lot of that canvas out?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I would imagine they would have dug it up and plowed it over.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the Indians, you know, they all had wild roots and stuff like that. They didn't have that kind of stuff, you know. They dressed in Indian clothes. And the Indian women used to have an old stove outside and they'd cook them at a barn farm and they'd have tree pods, you know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And those tree pods had iron over them and they'd roast those here canvases and they'd make flour out of that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: My can...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I think we better let this story go and tell you more about it at home, because I've got it all written out down there.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I recall quite a lot of work.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, it was. I raised 805 geese and I killed them all. Sold the feathers and sold the geese. And I like to raise them too. But I had to stay home all the time because the clouds were pouring white. It'd be a shower well, if the grass was just so high and the feet would get cold and the can would walk. So I'd have to pile them all in boxes and take them and dry them off. And then I'd take them out again. I'd raise about 35, 40 geese every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They can't walk?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, their legs would get cold, you know. And the grass would be so high and all that. And then they'd just get in a pile and I'd take them in the house and I'd dry them off, you see. That's why I had so many geese every year. I got pictures of them that I'd taken all different times. I used to like them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you keep them? Did you have them in a just all panned in, a small area?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the old chickens, see the old hen goos. Of course the old hen goos, I'd break her up and I'd set her eggs. And then the second batch, that's why she's had herself. And I had good luck with them. I always had good luck with the geese and I'd raise 35 or 40 every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you feed them, mostly?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, for 48 hours, I didn't feed them anything. Only water and sand and green grass. They'd lower the geese themselves and then after that, they'd make their own living then. They're about to keep the same size as your eggs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they didn't take grain or anything to keep them going?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, they made their own living after that. After two weeks, they made their own living. Only I just had to keep them dry, you know, watch them so the weather didn't get them too cold. And then the hens that hatched, I'd pour five eggs under a hen. And she'd raise them. And sometimes a chicken, wild -eyed chicken, I'd take care of. Let's see, I'd have five geese under a hen. And then the hen would take care of the chickens, the geese, you know. I'd turn to bed to do the geese with one chicken. And that's why I raised them. And then after the first straws, after all the pincers had grown out, I'd take them and get rid of the old hens then. I'd put them all together and they'd go by the flocks then and I'd have a place under the chicken house and I'd keep them there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Through the winter?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Till the witching time from the moon. When the first snow would fly, I'd start in then. And I'd start in running around for market prices and I'd get my prices and I'd write down how long they had had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More prices?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'd write for the prices then. The prices would begin to move then. But, oh, the turkey's not so good. The goose price is better around Thanksgiving. And I'd butcher them all. And every fourth boiler, I had two wash boilers, two number nine wash boilers and I had a place to do this work. And I had, let me see.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I had two wash boilers and I'd change the water every fourth boiler so the feathers don't get dirty and all and things like that. And then after that, I'd get them out and after all the geese were washed, I'd never sell one live weight, very seldom, because it didn't pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't pay to sell?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Live weight, no. No, it was better to sell. Well, it didn't pay all right, but it didn't make so much money, you know. I made more money on them by selling them live weight. And then I took feathers to sell and I had the geese to sell. That's how I made money on them. Otherwise, they make their own living. Geese are easier to raise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, would you tell me how you kill them? How was the best way to do that that you found?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I put them in a sack and then I'd

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: cut their shoulder, cut their shoulder and break their neck and then I'd have to breathe them and then some of them would get on the feathers. And I had nice green feathers and I'd grade them as I picked them. I had three kinds of feathers and so I sold feathers and geese the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How easy were the feathers to pick?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, you just, well, first I'd take them by the neck. After I'd kill them, I'd take them and I'd put them in the water first. I'd let the water come to a boil. I'd see it bubbling up and I'd pour the cold water in the boiler and then I'd put the goose in.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And I think I put them in the back first and then I'd throw the wings and then the wings were alright. Well, then last I put the neck in. I put the rest in and then the neck in. And that's the way I fixed them. And they're all nice. There was no blunge or anything. I had a place in Spokane. I shipped them to brothers. Every year I had geese to sell. And ducks. Oh my, I had lots of ducks. One time I shipped for an incubator and forty -four, forty eggs in the incubator. And I had, let me see, I had forty -four ducks.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I had to stop forty ducks. And the black ducks I'll find were the better duck to pick. And after the first snow flies, the first duck why, they'd be all pin feathers and everything. And I figured well, I'll clean them nice and you didn't have to draw them or anything them days. But now of course they're more strict and a lot of things like that. I'd ship them over to Seattle and Spokane and places like that. I was busy from the time, well I was, I was about seventy -eight half poultry in the wintertime on the holidays. Oh I liked it and it was profitable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say profitable, what was the going rate for the geese?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: The geese, them days you only get ten cents a pound.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That was butchered, that wasn't live weight.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That was live, butchered yes, it was live weight. And then after a while why, they'd give me, oh I can't say now. Well I sold them anyhow ten cents a pound dress. And the goose would bring about,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: well they only had two dollars when I was dressed. Oh you didn't make much on anything then. A lot of work.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You'd have to butcher them though all at once pretty much when you, when you write it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'd go to work and I'd butcher from, I'd dress from eight to eleven a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eight, what do you mean eight to eleven, eight and more into eleven at night?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, that many geese you know. Oh eight to eleven, yeah. I'd prepare a week ahead of time, I'd go get everything all ready. Get all my bread making and make all my bread and I'd make all my, everything ready. So I would just keep working, keep the clock going and all. I had a routine on the vine. So I got along just fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How, how, how many days would you, would you do it right one day after another?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, I'd get over it and take me about eight days. Eight and eight and nine, ten days. I'd ship away about, I'd see.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Actually took, took to work and took, I had enough pictures each year that I could frame it and put on the wall. So how many geese I'd raise each year. They were awful nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd do geese and ducks at the same time, the butcher?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'd butcher the geese first and then I'd butcher the ducks. And I'd wait a certain time before the ducks, the geese were ready and all. Because they were full of fin feathers, you know. And I'd wait till they were all fin feathers out and then I'd start in. And the duck would lose a pound a night in one night. You got to take to work and I'd let some, I'd just hide some down on one pasture and then I'd push, oh about half a dozen in another pasture. And that's why I'd butcher them. Because if they know you're butchering, they lose a pound a night. Oh yes, they're touchy. Not the geese, but the ducks. The ducks, yeah. They're a touch animal. And then you have to abandon ducks. And you don't want to abandon ducks in one bunch because they're frightened, you know, and they lose that weight. And you're out that much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they hard to catch when you wanted to catch them?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You got to raise them gently, you got to raise them with care, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then they're not afraid of you?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mm hm. And my goodness, I had 35, 40 ducks. And I had about 35 or 40 geese and put them both in the yard and they'd go out there and, oh my the noise. Oh it was just terrible. I guess the neighbors out there missed me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The neighbors, what did you say, the neighbors missed?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, my kind of racket, you know. Oh, that's funny. Yeast of all ducks, geese and ducks, most are noisy, but the drakes don't make as much noise as the hen dogs. Hen dogs make the noise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get used to that noise or did it over -see the shatters?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I didn't. I didn't pay no attention to it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I didn't pay no attention to it, I didn't get any noise or anything, I was used to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did these animals, the poultry, did you have to keep them pretty well penned up or did they have the run of the place?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: The coyotes are plentiful around all the country there and I'd come at night and in the morning I'd let them out and I'd watch them. That's the way I said I raised them. Then I saw the feathers for a dollar and a half, two and a half pounds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sell those to Spokane too?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I gave each girl a feather bed and two pair of pillows and then I had a feather mattress I made myself. I had two of them, a single one and a large one. And they're the nastiest things to work around, my goodness, they're down all over everything.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I can remember my wife making a sleeping bag out of down and having that same trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you go about making a feather mattress?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the best way to make them is I made eight sections, you know, you make eight sections and they go across that way. And I made eight sections, two eight section beds. And one side would be flat and the other side would be smooth, or biscuit -like. And I had those. I never liked a feather bed to sleep on, they're too cold in the winter time and they're too hot in the summer. That's why I never liked them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how could you keep the feathers down so that they didn't fly all over the place when you were trying to put them inside that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I worked with them when I went right away. And one time I had a sugar shack, you know, I'd take these sugar shacks and I'd sew them. I'd put them in the quarter, I think it is, in each sack. And then I'd hang them on the clothesline to dry. So one time I had a sugar shack, I had a little hole in it, I didn't think anything about it. I hung it on the line next to my bed and I had feathers from one end of the ranch to the other. The wind just took them through and they go through a little bit of a hole. And they went right straight through that wind, that streak, you know. And then took all my feathers away for that one year, my little one's sack. And the goose would produce a quarter of a pound. Less than a quarter of a pound.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, it's all hard work but I'm still a threat now.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, but to look back on it, to me, it's so interesting to know how people may do in those days, what they had to do. And what they enjoyed doing too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, I liked to work with poultry, I always did like to work with poultry. I had the time on every mile that you can feathers at all. I'd just say, fool with the geese. Because before you geese you know, you'd bring all the feathers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you then sell most all the feathers?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I had eleven orders for feathers for when I quit. But I couldn't fill them. And one woman gets to honor a mother like she didn't understand how much work I did. She'd have to crank it because I, well she wasn't, they were all extra feathers you know. So one woman got to honor her.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She just wanted the feathers to make something, to make pillows. Yeah, to make pillows for herself.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: But people used these rubber bands, not rubber pillows. Right. And those of us that had allergies too, they actually used just rubber. So that's it. But I can tell the difference because, you know, when they're sitting on the bed, I just touch them and I can tell what's in them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, did you get attached to the geese that you had?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, there was some I did. Oh yeah, I got kind of attached to them, but you get so, you get so, you have so much of that stuff you know, that it's just, you know, you get rid of them. And Bud always used to say, I don't say how you kill them things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bud, your husband?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I don't say how you kill them things. Oh, I said, they're just a bird.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're just a bird?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it bother you with the birds that you liked to have to kill them?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I kind of hated them. I found a way to kill them. You just cut them in the throat and then break their neck you know. Then they don't know anything. And one day I had the worst time, he weighed 17 pounds, and I had the worst time with him to get him to die. I don't know why, but he was so hard to, and I would throw him up in the wagon box and then he would lay there and I thought he was dead, and by gosh he was still alive and I want to get him. I'd say there was blood in my blood and I'd give it to those chickens and the geese and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get a picture of a goose?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, it was a whole lot of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your husband help you with this process?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh no.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I don't know, I don't think he ever pictured a goose or a duck. He just didn't like them. Then he'd get in the watering trough and I'd go and put a needle in the boards around the top of the watering trough. They couldn't reach in there and get the dirty geese in the water and get the watering trough dirty. I had to take care of that. So I went to work and I put a board in the nursery washtub and I took and put a board in that and that's when the end of the board sticking in the water and sticking out and then I put a rock in there. So they didn't waste the water then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that the ducks got real touchy and they'd lose weight if they knew that they were going to be killed, do you think they were smarter than the geese?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I don't think so. I don't hardly think so. But he wouldn't eat any ate a goose or a duck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wouldn't eat them? Why not?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I don't know, he didn't like them. Children and I liked them and everybody else did but he never liked them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he went back east to Pennsylvania to visit and somebody said, Oh my, they had a lovely dinner and special dinner and I said, guess what they had?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They read me a big letter of duck. They thought I had something special. Because it was funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wrote you this in the letter?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah. Duck.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, I was like a roast duck. My dad was like, the whole family liked ducks and all. It takes about three and a half hours to roast them. But these hunters think it's terrible. You need so much time to roast a duck. But I like them done when I go to cook them while I'm done. And not too fat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they're better than chicken as a meat?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I don't know, it's just a variety. And they have variety purposes.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I've always liked duck a lot. I've always considered it a delicacy to have it because you don't get them much. I guess not, no.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: But I always liked duck film.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And that was for snow flies. And mating time too. Meat is stringy, it isn't good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And mating time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: At least the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do they mate?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: A lot from, well, turbo rayon, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When would the young be born?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: It takes 28 days to hatch a duck, a goose.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And 26 days for a duck.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That's what it took.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the babies take a lot of care?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, you gotta stay home, you can't run around. You're raiding geese and ducks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you couldn't trust the flock to take care of the babies?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh no, but the geese had, old gander, not a hen goose or such. She would cover 12 to 14 eggs. Yes, and the old gander too, she was a little proud. But you break them up the first setting. And then they start laying the second time, and then you can raise two batches of geese, so that way you see. That's the way I did it. Oh, I'd have so much pull range stuff around the place, goodness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you let some of the hens and the ganders, now the ganders, would you let them live more than one year?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, they had to be more than one year old. Yes, because they're better geese, they can raise geese, but no relation. And they should be a year old when you get them. And they should be taken care of. No relation at all. I used to swap with them, you know, people at times, again, and sometimes they'd bring me some of the darndest geese because they used to make me mad. And geese would be just like anything else, where we'd pasture them or we'd pasture them, it would go to the meat. You mean it's no good when they... Oh, it tastes ugly, you know. One time, Mr. Farr, I was raising such nice geese, so one of the neighbors, he thought he would eat it, and he was kind of a cheapskate anyhow and so on. He says, I want a gander, so save one for me and I'll trade with you. So my trader, he wanted a dollar to two dollars. That's what he did, and he hollered because of charging two dollars for the gander. Well, he was just a cheapskate, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the matter with these birds that were inferior, that weren't as good as yours, and what would make them worse?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, nothing would make them worse, otherwise they were inferior because they didn't have the right pasture. You know, you and I were pasturing apples and chops. I'd finish them off with apples and chops and that would make them nice and fine grainy. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would you put them on that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say some of these, like these birds that came that weren't any good, what kind of pasture were they on, you think?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, pig pasture, I think, or something. I never would trade with that fellow again. We just saw five, I just did it for accommodation purposes, that's all.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Sure, you kind of have to do that kind of thing. Yeah, we neighborly.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people raise geese and ducks around Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, they don't like to bother, they stay home long enough.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You know, I wanted to ask you about the community on Cow Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, were most of the people there, what was their cultural background? Were they Germans?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, they're just like any place else, just all kinds here, they just get it over and they just call them Cow Creek. Cow Creek was east of Genesee and Thorn Creek was west of Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you were on Thorn Creek.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, we were just halfway between my folks' homestead and that's the way they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many Catholic people around there besides you?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes, goodness, yes. First one was Catholics and Father Cataldo used to stay at the granddad's and help take care of the children, help raise them. And he helped grandfathers and he was helpful to those children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean Father Cataldo actually helped raise the kids?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, he says he's stuck over the place and then he'd go down over to Lewiston Hill, he'd walk down Lewiston Hill a lot of times. One time, I'll remember my... Oh, he's hanging on it. Yeah, these are open.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, these are open?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, he grew up and he'd come there and he'd stay overnight and he'd have a bachelor. They'd sit and talk and visit and everything and I don't think they paid car. And grandfather, he said he'd go to Iron Kettle, you know, pop out Iron Kettle. And he'd have that full of water and he'd make that full of rolled wheat, you know. And he'd make that full of marsh and then he'd get the syrup. They'd go up to Palouse and they'd get their supply of groceries in the folder here. And he used to go down there and get all these groceries at Palouse, Washington from Thorn Creek.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And he'd have all the syrup and everything else and he'd have these jackets, what they call jackets, you know, you'd see no jacket syrup. You don't see that anymore.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They're tin and then they have tin on their side and then they have wood on the outside. And they hold from two to three gallon size and he'd always have several of them sitting around. Then by midnight they'd go to their lunch and use that on their syrup. They'd use the syrup on their cereal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long would Father Cataldo stop over there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, until around the territory. He'd sit prayers and masses on the open prairie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would he hold the services?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Right in the open service.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right outside our doors?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, right outside. And that's the way he held the services.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he'd stay with your grandfather maybe for a couple of weeks at a time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, he'd stay there and he'd batch him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your grandfather wasn't married at that time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, his wife was dead. Oh, that's right. She'd already died. So Father Cataldo would stay for a long time. That's so interesting to me. That's so interesting. Yeah, he'd stay there and he'd help the cook and everything like that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And some mother, she was 11 years old and he urged her to take her up to the De Smet Mission. And there she stayed five years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he personally took care of her up there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was kind of a bigger, it was a fairly big place at that time? De Smet Mission?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That was a school up there made by the Sisters of Providence, a charity. And he used to go up there and Mother said that Grandpa put her in the cart. They had them two -wheeled carts, you know, and he used to travel around the country and all to say prayers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a horse too?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No. They called him Soca. He took Mother on that and took them three days to go from Tharquik up to De Smet Mission. No kidding. And rained all the way up. They stopped Catholic places. People in them days were more different than they are now. And so she stayed there at De Smet Mission and she never seen her home for three years or five years. I don't know which it was now.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That's the way she went to school up there then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you think it would be better for her to go up there than to stay at home with her father?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, well she'd be better off because there were so many. Well, there were three brothers and then other men that would come in and stop there. There were too many men around and he thought it would be the best place for her. He wanted her to learn how to cook and do things like that. She learned lots of nice things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like when you say nice things, what kinds of things did she learn?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She learned how to dress make and how to make patterns and how to dress them and sew and do hand work. I don't know. She just did everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty isolated on Thorn Creek at that time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: The whole country was isolated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But she didn't have the chance down there to learn?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would she have had much chance to go to school down on Thorn Creek?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, they had a nice school down there. The little school district. Granddad was on the school district all them years. And she stayed there with, so he took her up to the cement mission. And she didn't get to see home men for four years I guess or three years. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she think of the Indians up there? Did she talk about?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the Indians, the royal sisters had up there. They had the Indian girls in one part of the place and the white girls in the other part. And that's the way they kept them and all. And in the church the same way. They'd go in the church, they'd be in the church in the same way. And they'd do it at the eating house the same way too. The Indian sisters would teach the sisters. And one time Mother said that she didn't take very good care of her and she got sick up there. I think she took chicken parmigiana, she was a meezer or something.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And then she got deaf and she blamed it on to that. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did she get deaf? Was it much later?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes. Well, I could have an effect on you I think. Oh yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She took cold because the building that they were using took fire and burn. So they had nothing. So they had to sleep on straw. So they got along alright but she never died or anything. But so got sick. But she got along alright though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She slept in a barn then or something?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Or an old house. No, it was an old building. Uh huh. So that's all there was to it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: But I wonder where the other girls like her, the white girls came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder did they just come from around and work out?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: All come from around.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: During the holidays there was Corpus Christi and I can nail the different holidays. And on there would be a big holiday where they all gather up their teepees and all their stuff and they'd go up there to the Smith Mission. And they'd have church services for a few days. That's the way they lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she spend much time at all with the Indians up there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, there were a lot of white girls up there. White girls in Liberty Lake now. Liberty, Stephen Liberty. That was named and then Stevens Pass. And let's see, Stevens Pass. Anyway, those places were named after those, after the squaw men. They called them squaw men. They married Indian women because they could get some land that way to farm. So they had a little land and they'd farm and take it along some way I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't there quite a few squaw men around Genesee too?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yeah, there was squaw men. And they called that the reservation out east of Genesee. And that's where the Indian people, that's where the squaw men were living.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I've heard it said that a lot of times people didn't think too much of the squaw men. No, they didn't like them at all. They said, oh there he goes, he's a squaw men. That's the way they talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that was? You think they figured that they were making it easy, too easy to get the land?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, other ones would get land. They didn't want to get the land and they'd get out there and farm it. That's why they wanted to get married squabs.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'm wondering why people didn't care for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why they didn't think they were as good?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They didn't like Indian women because they were taking up the life in Genesee.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And I guess the land too, they held a lot of the land too. Oh, the prairie.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I remember when they had our farm down there. Dad had a ton or forty acres and he'd always did it. And he had a prairie and lots of school section land. So he had three pieces of land and he got his land that way.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He had 240 acres when you were growing up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's how much land?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that about as much as a person could handle in those days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He could have had 600 to 1 ,000 acres if he wanted it. So he said he was afraid he couldn't handle it. He said, I wish I had taken more now, but I want to know now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because of the equipment that he had?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, and then he broke his leg up from his stack and that settled everything. He had a hard time. He raised lots of hogs.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Do you think that meant, that must have meant he would have had to work a lot harder than another man who could get around more easy. Oh yes, he had paid the rent for 27 years.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Did he ever, did he ever, I mean, did he have a hard, do you think, a hard time to get the work done or did he think he could do it just as...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh no, he had a plow that when he broke his leg, of course he would have put three horses on a walking or riding plow. And then he always had a foot burner, what they call a foot burner. And a man always had three horses on that, so he got his land farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he had a hard hand at the price?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the hard hand stay there the whole year round?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Most of the time, he was there most of the time. Dad had a lot of cattle in. I thought he had a cattle and... Oh, he had hogs and horses and everything else. He'd have made it good and he'd have been well -to -do. He had a college education and he'd have been well -to -man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If he'd had a college education?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes. He actually broke his leg and I was just giving him a little sit back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever get to go to college at all?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this hired man, was it hard to keep a hired man?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, he was a darn cranky. Sometimes I didn't want to stay. He'd have to get a middle -aged man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would you be able to keep a man?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I don't know. Sometimes he'd keep a man, but then again, it just depends on him, you know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Did they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: would the hired men get to be a real part of the family or did they stay pretty separate?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, they stayed pretty separate.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He had a big long trench and he'd put troughs over there to fill it full of water and put potatoes in there and then he'd... I don't know why he'd put that wheat on top of that and that would last a while. That's what he had in his hogs and his shipping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember the first work that you did around the house? Some of the first work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, goodness. We started in pretty young. I don't know. We started in pretty young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, when you were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what were you expected to do as you were growing up?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I remember we did the regular housework routine. We did the regular housework. I can't remember.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We also flew horses and cattle.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You told me there was some... you were talking before, I think you mentioned that you had to help him with the pigs when you were just a kid.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yeah, sure. And you said that was rough. That was kind of rough work. Whether it be cold, you know, when we had two dogs and the hogs would get away and I'd have to go ask for them and get them home again. Just a lot of work. That's hard work.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You know, you said you moved into Genesee. You were still pretty young.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you then?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I was about 12, 13 years old.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You mentioned the drummers. Seeing those drummers come into your father's hotel and I was wondering about what those fellows were like.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, those fellows were just traveling salesmen, that's all. Then lots of times they'd have a team of horses and they'd... or a single buggy and all, they'd take their samples of the farmers and sometimes they'd buy stuff and sometimes they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did those drummers dress up real fancy and put on airs?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, they were complicated.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Some of them were complicated.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They couldn't expect too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Too much from what? From people?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, and too much, you know, fancy food and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father's hotel do very big business in those days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, they didn't until the train came into Lewiston and then they settled it. That killed Genesee.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Until...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: that was about 1898, right?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: If I'm not mistaken.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Genesee was going to be the capital of Idaho one time, but I don't know, somebody got in and stole papers or something in Genesee, so they got them down to Boise and they... They got the patents all out of this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your father sold his farm when you moved into town? He held on to it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He held on to it,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: but he bought the hotel though.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, he must have been doing pretty well if he could afford to buy the hotel too. Yeah, he did alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. I was going to ask you about the Rosenstein's too and what they were like. I understand they were Jewish people.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, Jake, you never worry about getting his money. I guess it's $4 ,000 on the books. But he never let anybody go in the world. He's been so for a year or so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean to his family? What do you mean to his family, huh?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Of course.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They had nice children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they real well accepted in the Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He had a couple of daughters and a son. He had three daughters. He was Gussie and Hattie and Irma and Mavi and then... And Mox. Mox. Mox. He used to be all the same as Mark. My goodness, he was crazy with that guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mark's son?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, and he had a store down at LaVoy. And it doesn't mean the way he put him up there to run the store. I expect him to run the store. Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his son still pretty young when he was doing that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He had a store up there on Monroe Street.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Army several store. And then he done pretty good. Then his health was gonna fail him and he got married. And his daughter, he said he hoped he lived till his daughter got married. Then he died. So he was over nice when Mark was. But all those things were all severe. I was that boy.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Monroe Street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that in Spokane?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Mark or the old man that had the store up there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mark did.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, are any of his children still living?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I think they're all in Los Angeles now. I used to see Bobby. I corresponded with her for a long time. And I corresponded with the younger girl.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And they all died, I guess. They all were nice children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which, who did you know best? Was one of them, was Bobby a pretty close friend of yours?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I was funny as well. Well, baby, she was kind of snippy, but the rest of her was nice to me. Harry, she was a bookkeeper secretary. She hasn't still panned for a long time. I don't know, she went to Seattle and then, uh, Gussie. She went down to Los Angeles, I think.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And I don't remember much about them now. I can't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you think that, uh, that people, um, that people thought about their being Jewish at all?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mm. They're German Jews, Pennsylvania Dutch.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That's her old shoes on a nice woman. She doesn't have property down there yet. About 400 acres.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: What was Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rosenstein, um, like?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, she's another nice woman. She was so Americanized. And old Jake was Jewish, just Jewish, you know, old country Jewish. Or, I don't know, or, or, but she was more and more Americanized than he was. But he was always good to us children.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I thought you were saying he was kind of mean to them too, though. Yeah, he was sometimes. But he was good to them at the same time sometimes, too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: People took advantage of him, charging up a lot of the books. That was awful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because he was kind -hearted?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was money like that? Was that money, a lot of it, he never collected?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, there's a lot of the stores in Genesee. Lots of money. That's why they crooked people down there. Mr. goodness, they're going to buy stuff and they're never going to tend to pay for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could that go, could somebody get away with that for a very long time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, they had my thing. You can't do anything. The food is gone, that's it. They had no security.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I would think a family, or a family was doing that, they wouldn't be too welcome in the country for once they were found.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, the things are on different now. No, they're on different now.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They don't owe this credit anymore. It's a good thing too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that a family, say a family did that, would they still be able to stay around Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No. Because that's what I started this year, non -credit business of course. I always have to pay cash for everything. I must have something traded in, eggs and butter and vegetation and all that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, people do different now.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And the tricks I saw, do they go work and go in the store and buy things and trade stuff in and they'd buy stuff and the stuff they take would be inferior. And the person that bought them got stung on them and that's the way they did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that kind of thing was rare or common in those days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, it was just a common thing.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: But I would think good families would never do something like that. No.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You see, they had stuff down there and they'd pay a big price for it and the person would know the bottle and drop out. And then there'd be the merchant with that high price stuff on his hands and no sale for it and that's the way it went. Did that happen to... I was going to Roselstein, Maryland.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And Roselstein was always great for that little fish, you know, Norwegians and Scandinavians used to go there and they'd get that little fish, you know. And it was just like an old piece of wood I guess. It would take about a week to get it cured but always as good after it's cured though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when Roselstein set up his son in Laughaway, is that something that his son didn't really want to do?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, he didn't like to do it very well. We had to go down there and batch and run the store too and poor Mark had a hard time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was still young pretty much?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'm sure he had when he was a kid. And then he'd have to look for himself and do all that stuff like that. Kids didn't have the days to have the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it made a difference to the parents if their daughters married Jewish boys or not?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh no, nobody said anything. There was, Roselstein's I guess was about the only Jews, Levi and Grostein too, they were Jews, they were in Millicent. But they were starting in Genesee, the old Genesee was across the track. Levi and Grostein had a store there and they moved across and then they let that go and they had in the store their own. And it took three months to move the store building from across the track over where they settled down later on now. And there's where the community building sits.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know who was the cause of them moving, who stopped the railroad?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Somebody tried to hold them up for...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh they had trouble, they couldn't get to the land they wanted and they had trouble, my goodness. So, Roselstein had a piece of land picture out there on one side of the track.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: See how it is now. Roselstein, he wanted so much for the land and so they picture the land on this side of the track then. And there's where they started Genesee then on this side of the track, that's why it's on the other side. Then they got to the old town, new town. Well was it Roselstein that was holding up the railroad for the... Yeah, holding a big price and they called it, took him a few months to went to the store over there.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I can't imagine, I wonder why Roselstein would, it seems like he only hurt himself by holding up the railroad since he had to move his store.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well sure, but he didn't have no sense, you know. I don't know what the world was about anyhow. I've cut a story out about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was I thinking?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I was going to ask you about, about J .P. Vollmer too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well that stinker, he was just terrible. He had that store there, you know, that store building up there. And he had the bank and he took from, this is the actual truth about him. And nobody had any use for him. He had that store there and had merchandise and had everything in his own hand. And he'd urge people to get everything they wanted, you know. Says you can pay for it this fall. Well when Paul come, it rained and rained and... My, that was a rainy season, nobody saved their crops, they lost all they had and they took their farms and everything. That was in 93. That's because they were in debt to him for a free store. Yeah, he urged them to buy things, you know. And didn't give them a chance to pay for it. He told them he'd give them a chance, he told them they'd get credit. Yeah, he gave them credit and everything. And then they were like, I guess he didn't own the different deal and then the weather was bad and he...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Took their property away from them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this happen to, did many people lose their land to a farmer at that time?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You could always tell the farmer's place and he was going along the...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Going along the roads, you know, when the automobiles come. Always could tell the farmer's place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the barn and the... No house with a barn, you know. He looks at the barn and then the... It'd be just the barn set there, that's all. And some places there was a house. And some people had letters. They had on their backs and they worked out the message.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Left the country? But you could tell it was a farmer's place because there was usually no house on it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yeah, sure.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And the barn, you know, was set on the field someplace. And there would be no roads, you see. They didn't have no roads established yet.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So he held on to the land after he got it. Yeah. I guess my understanding has been that people did not like him at all around here. Oh, my, no. He used to meet him. I don't see why you're giving people a chance.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And then Mother said they were running a hotel and they had the ranch rented out and... She says they were running a hotel and the farmer and his son come in there and the weather gets bad and they couldn't save the crowd. So I thought they both come in there and dressed in overalls and try to save what they could save.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Vollmer came and dressed in overalls?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, and there's some JP.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, that's what he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this after 1893? This was later then?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah. Yes, this was all during the year of 1993. Oh, that was the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was trying to save his own crops?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yeah, sure. Well, he didn't stay around Genesee after that. No, he stayed in Lewiston. He was a Jew.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He didn't know the Jew.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So he got out of the country. Yeah, he got to stay in Lewiston.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, you know people around Troy hated him too. So they changed their name from Vollmer to Troy. That was called Vollmer to start where the town of Troy was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was, no?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, it was until... Well, Troy is one part of Idaho and Troy is Lake Tooele County and the other part of... the southern part of Idaho, that was Miss First County.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I had all kinds of trouble.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: It just sounds like...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: ...93 was maybe the worst year. Well, you see the other guy, they were... ...then they was ahead of the grain, you see. And lots of them bombed, so mostly hitters. And they'd have to wait for the other fellow to come with the machinery and everything like that. And that's why they lost their...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: ...everything. And they had nothing left. They had a head, a crease grew in the heads while they were standing in the field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So because they headed it, then they had to stack it? Is that right?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Instead of bind it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, they put it in three to five stacks. Two to five stacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two to five stacks for a whole farm?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, for the farm, you know, for all the grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For 160 acres?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, for a well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or even more?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, take more grain than that. More cooked grain to mix. Five stacks.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Take them about a week, ten days. And the weather was different. The atmosphere and everything is so different than it is now, I don't know. Seems like the weather's changed and the country isn't like it was.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, you said that the air was pure around, in Genesee. Yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: There wasn't so many people and so many, you know, so they could have populated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How different, how was the weather itself different then?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, the weather was all right. Only of course they had that deep snow, you know. They had to take their horses and come to Genesee and drive right over the barbed wire fences. They had so much snow. And then mud, why, it was awful.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: It was pretty rough to get to travel through that mud. Oh, yes. The wheels would be up deep in the mud. In the main street of Genesee there was nothing but mud. They had mud there, my goodness, it was terrible... You had the six to eight horse, no, it was...
